RAG+CoI,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Based on the provided text chunks, Pharo is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" because it does not provide type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns. This means that the type of an object (which is considered to be its class) is not explicitly defined, but rather determined at runtime.

Pharo's syntax is designed to be simple and minimal, with only a few keywords and a focus on sending messages to objects. This syntax is intended to be easy to read and understand, even for those unfamiliar with the language.

In Pharo, everything happens by sending messages to objects. This means that objects are responsible for their own actions, and the method associated with the message is looked up dynamically. When sending a message, the receiver of the message (the object) decides how to respond, rather than the sender.
Pharo's syntax is based on the concept of sending messages to objects. The language does not ""invoke methods"" but rather sends messages to objects, which then respond accordingly.
The syntax of Pharo is minimal, with only six keywords and no syntax for control structures or declaring new classes. Instead, nearly everything is achieved by sending messages to objects.

Pharo is a reflective language, which means that it is able to reflect on its own execution and structure. This allows for introspection and intercession, which enables the modification of the language semantics and the behavior of a program from within the program itself.

The syntax of Pharo is designed to be simple and easy to read, with a focus on sending messages to objects. The language does not provide type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns, which is why it is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"".

RAG+CoI,"What is the difference between ""instance Variable Names"" and ""class Variable Names"", in pharo?","

I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser.
I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).

,

I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.
Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.
Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them ""pool variables"", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a ""class variable"" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
,Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to your question:

In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself. This means that two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods. There is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object.

Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. This means that Pharo instance variables are similar to protected variables in C and Java. However, we prefer to say that they are private, because it is considered bad style in Pharo to access an instance variable directly from a subclass.

Class variables are shared between instances and classes. Each class has its own private class instance variables, and a subclass will inherit those class instance variables but have its own private copies. Class variables are shared amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself. Class variables are similar to shared variables in other languages, but they are not exactly the same.

Class variables are used to share data amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself. This is useful when you need to share some data amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself.

Class instance variables and class methods are different from ordinary instance variables and methods in that they operate on different objects (classes in this case). Class instance variables describe the state of classes (instances of metaclasses), and class methods describe the behavior of classes.

In summary, instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself, while class variables are shared between instances and classes. Class instance variables describe the state of classes, and class methods describe the behavior of classes.",314,,,,,1.0,0.8808794705037325,12,"['Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are RAG+CoI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.

,A method in Pharo always answers a message with a value, which is an object. This value is defined by the `^` construct in the method, but if the method reaches the end without executing a `^`, it still answers the object that received the message. This is known as returning `self`, as `self` represents the receiver of the message.

When a method is called, the virtual machine starts looking for the method in the receiver's class, and then up the inheritance chain. If the method is not found, it sends the message `doesNotUnderstand:` to the receiver, which then looks for the method in its superclass, and so on.

In Pharo, everything happens by sending messages. Even class methods are dynamic, and are looked up in the same way as other methods. This means that class methods can be overridden in subclasses, just like instance methods.
The `^` construct is used to return a value from a method. If a method reaches the end without executing a `^`, it returns `self`, as mentioned earlier.

The uniformity of Pharo's object model means that classes are just regular objects, and behave like any other object. This means that classes can be sent messages, and can respond to those messages by sending messages themselves.

In Pharo, there is no difference between sending a message to an object and sending a message to a class. The method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, and then proceeds up the inheritance chain.
The rules of Pharo's object model are:
2. Every object is an instance of a class.
5. Method lookup follows the inheritance chain.

These rules are applied uniformly throughout the system, which means that there is no magic or special cases.",294,,,,,0.65,0.7718411248873948,13,"['self Actually a method always answers a message with a value ( which is , of course , an object ) .', 'Object class Uniformity between Classes and Objects It is interesting to step back a moment and realize that there is no difference between sending a message to an object and to a class .', 'Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .', 'Rule 4 Everything happens by sending messages .', 'If the method is not found in the class of the receiver , it is looked up in its superclasses .', '* Every object is an instance of a class .', 'Everything is an object .', '16.2 Revisiting the Pharo object model Rule 1 .', 'A method that terminates without explicitly returning some expression will implicitly return self .', 'In both cases the search for the corresponding method starts in the class of the receiver , and proceeds up the inheritance chain .', 'Method lookup dynamically follows the inheritance chain .', 'Rule 3 Every class has a superclass .', 'However , the uniformity of the Pharo object model ( where classes are just regular objects ) means that they are somewhat different : whereas Java static methods are really just statically - ,The question is how to check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo.
The relevant text chunks from the book ""Pharo By Example"" are:

- The class Set is a collection which behaves as a mathematical set, i.e., as a collection with no duplicate elements and without any order. In a Set, elements are added using the message add: and they cannot be removed. The message size returns the number of elements in the Set. The message isEmpty returns true if the Set is empty.
- The message includes: anObject returns true if the Set contains the given anObject.
- You can use the message includes: anObject to check if an element already exists in a set.

- The message includes: anObject can also be used with a SortedCollection, which is a collection that maintains its elements in a sorted order. The message includes: anObject returns true if the SortedCollection contains the given anObject.

- The message includes: anObject can also be used with a Set, which is a collection that maintains its elements in a set, i.e., without any duplicates. The message includes: anObject returns true if the Set contains the given anObject.

column1:
RAG+CoI,Message Not Understood for existing method,"

Ok, so it's like this:
Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?

,

You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

,The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is executed when an object does not understand a message. This method is a part of the Object protocol and is responsible for handling messages that are not understood by an object. When an object receives a message that it does not understand, it sends itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the message selector as its argument. This process is repeated until the `doesNotUnderstand:` method is found, at which point the debugger is launched.

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is a convenient way to intercept messages that are not understood by an object. This method can be overridden by subclasses of Object to provide alternative behavior for handling messages that are not understood. Two common applications of this technique are to implement lightweight proxies for objects and to dynamically compile or load missing code.

In Pharo, when an object receives a message, it first looks in the method dictionary of its class for a corresponding method to respond to the message. If no such method exists, it will continue looking up the class hierarchy, until it reaches Object. If still no method is found for that message, the object will send itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the message selector as its argument. The process then starts all over again, until the `doesNotUnderstand:` method is found, and the debugger is launched.

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is commonly abbreviated as DNU or MNU and is sent whenever message lookup fails. The default implementation of this method is to trigger the debugger at this point. It may be useful to override `doesNotUnderstand:` to provide some other behavior.
In Pharo, messages are understood as follows:
The order in which messages are sent is determined by the type of message. There are just three types of messages: unary, binary, and keyword messages.

When an object receives a message, it first looks in the method dictionary of its class for a corresponding method to respond to the message. If no such method exists, it will continue looking up the class hierarchy, until it reaches Object. If still no method is found for that message, the object will send itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message with the message selector as its argument.

,To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you can use the following approach:
1. Convert the Dictionary into an OrderedCollection using the `withAll:` message.
2. Then, you can sort the OrderedCollection using the `asSortedCollection` message.
This will give you an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order.
According to the Pharo documentation, the `withAll:` message creates a new instance of the receiving class, and then adds all elements of the given collection to this new instance.

In this case, `aPluggableDictionary withAll` creates a new instance of `OrderedCollection` and adds all the Rankable objects to it. Then, `asSortedCollection` sorts the OrderedCollection in ascending order based on the values of the Rankable objects.
You can also use the `valuesDo:` message to iterate over the values of the Dictionary, and then sort them in ascending order.
This will give you an OrderedCollection with the values of the Rankable objects in ascending order.

Note that the `asSortedCollection` message sorts the collection based on the natural order of the elements. In this case, the natural order is the ascending order of the values.",244,,,,,0.1666666666666666,0.6214074789247394,1,"['* To sort a collection , send it the message asSortedCollection .']","['Then , you can sort the OrderedCollection using the ` asSortedCollection ` message .', 'that the ` asSortedCollection ` message sorts the collection based on the natural order of the elements']",212,5593,447,5,"['What message is sent when the result of (OrderedCollection withAll: #(4 5 6 1 2 3)) asSortedCollection is given to a class?', 'What are some of the key collection classes in Pharo?', 'What are the most generic ways to create instances of collections in Smalltalk?', 'What is a SortedCollection in Pharo called?', 'What is ""OrderedCollection withAll"" in Pharo?']",pharo,llama3:instruct

Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

,

If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.
If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.
Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.
If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
,To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can redefine the equality method (=) in the Array class. This method should compare the elements of the two arrays, not their identities.

In Pharo, the default implementation of object equality is to test for object identity. However, in some cases, you might want to override this method to test for object equality instead. For example, you might want to consider two complex numbers equal if they have the same real and imaginary parts, even if they are different objects.
Here is an example of how you can redefine the equality method for arrays:
and: [self at:1 to: self size do: [:e | anArray at:1 to: anArray size detect: [:a | a = e] thenNot: [^ false]]]]

This method checks if the two arrays have the same size, and then checks if the elements at the same indices in the two arrays are equal. If both conditions are true, it returns true, indicating that the two arrays contain the same elements.
You can also use the following method to compare the contents of two arrays:
and: [self at:1 to: self size do: [:e | (anArray at:1 to: anArray size detect: [:a | a = e] then: [^ false])]]]

This method is similar to the previous one, but it returns a boolean value indicating whether the two arrays contain the same elements.",265,,,,,0.1666666666666666,0.5904933551665549,1,['The default implementation of object equality is to test for object identity : This is a method that you will frequently want to override .'],"['However , in some cases , you might want to override this method to test for object equality instead .', 'In Pharo , the default implementation of object equality is to test for object identity .']",238,4397,342,2,"['How is object equality determined in Smalltalk?', 'In what way do these collections handle their elements?']",pharo,llama3:instruct

Return zero-based index in the list of the first item whose value is equal to x. Raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

The optional arguments start and end are interpreted as in the slice notation and are used to limit the search to a particular subsequence of the list. The returned index is computed relative to the beginning of the full sequence rather than the start argument.

In Python 3.x, iteritems() was replaced with simply items(), which returns a set-like view backed by the dict, like iteritems() but even better.
This is also available in 2.7 as viewitems().

The operation items() will work for both 2 and 3, but in 2 it will return a list of the dictionary's (key, value) pairs, which will not reflect changes to the dict that happen after the items() call. If you want the 2.x behavior in 3.x, you can call list(d.items()).

,In Python, when you iterate over a dictionary using a `for` loop, you're actually iterating over the dictionary's keys. The syntax `for key in d:` is a shorthand way of saying ""for each key in the dictionary `d`, do something"".

What's happening behind the scenes is that Python is converting the dictionary into an iterable object, which is a special type of object that allows you to iterate over its elements. In this case, the iterable object is a dictionary view object, which is a proxy object that provides a read-only view of the dictionary's keys.

When you iterate over the dictionary view object, Python returns each key in the dictionary, one at a time. The `key` variable takes on the value of each key in the dictionary, and you can use it to access the corresponding value in the dictionary using `d[key]`.

So, to answer your question, `key` is not a special keyword, but rather a variable that takes on the value of each key in the dictionary. Python recognizes that it needs to read only the keys from the dictionary because the `for` loop is explicitly iterating over the dictionary's keys, not its values.

,The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is a common idiom in Python that determines whether the current script is being run as the main program or being imported as a module. Here's what it does:

When a Python script is run directly (e.g., `python myscript.py`), the special variable `__name__` is set to `""__main__""`. This is because the interpreter is running the script as the main program. However, when the same script is imported as a module (e.g., `import myscript`), the `__name__` variable is set to the name of the module (e.g., `""myscript""`).

The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement checks whether the current script is being run as the main program (i.e., `__name__` is equal to `""__main__""`). If it is, the code indented under the `if` statement will be executed.
You should include this statement in your Python scripts for several reasons:

1. **Prevent code from running accidentally**: When you import a Python module, you might not want the code in that module to run automatically. By wrapping your main program code in an `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` block, you ensure that it only runs when the script is executed directly, not when it's imported as a module.

2. **Allow for testing and reuse**: By separating your main program code from the rest of the module, you can test and reuse the module's functionality without running the main program code accidentally.
3. **Improve code organization**: The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` block helps to organize your code by separating the main program logic from the rest of the module's functionality.
In the example you provided, the code inside the `if` block (`print(""Hello, World!"")`) will only run when the script is executed directly, not when it's imported as a module.

In summary, the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is a way to ensure that your Python script's main program code runs only when the script is executed directly, not when it's imported as a module. It's a good practice to include this statement in your Python scripts to keep your code organized and reusable.",333,,,,,0.1,0.6023005818781917,1,"['If it is true , the indented statement runs .']","['If it is , the code indented under the ` if ` statement will be executed .']",260,1330,476,0,[],python,llama3:instruct

Explanation: the methods based on + (including the implied use in sum) are, of necessity, O(L**2) when there are L sublists -- as the intermediate result list keeps getting longer, at each step a new intermediate result list object gets allocated, and all the items in the previous intermediate result must be copied over (as well as a few new ones added at the end). column2: total number of copies is M times the sum of x for x from 1 to L excluded, i.e., M * (L**2)/2.

To flatten a list of lists, you can use a simple list comprehension with a generator expression:
flat_list = [item for sublist in your_list_of_lists for item in sublist]

,

To use a global variable that was defined in one function inside other functions, you simply need to access it as you would any other global variable. No additional declarations are needed.
x = 20  # creates a local variable x

In this example, we define a global variable `x` and two functions `func1` and `func2`. `func1` simply prints the global variable `x`, while `func2` creates a local variable `x` and prints it. Despite the local variable `x` in `func2`, the global variable `x` remains unchanged.

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. ,In Python, the `yield` keyword is used to define a generator. A generator is a special type of iterable, like a list or a tuple, but it can be used to generate a sequence of values on-the-fly, rather than storing them all in memory at once.

When a function contains a `yield` statement, it becomes a generator function. When the function is called, it returns a generator object, which is an iterator that can be used to iterate over the values yielded by the function.

In the example code you provided, the `_get_child_candidates` method is a generator function. When it is called, it returns a generator object that can be used to iterate over the child candidates yielded by the method.

In this specific case, the `_get_child_candidates` method yields the `_leftchild` and `_rightchild` attributes of the node, if they exist and satisfy certain conditions. The method is designed to be used in a loop, where the caller iterates over the generator object and processes each yielded value.

When the method is called, it does not return a list or a single element. Instead, it returns a generator object that can be used to iterate over the child candidates. The method is designed to be used in a loop, where the caller iterates over the generator object and processes each yielded value.

Subsequent calls to the method will stop when the generator object is exhausted, which means that all possible child candidates have been yielded. The method will not continue to yield values indefinitely.

To illustrate this, let's consider an example. Suppose we call the `_get_child_candidates` method and it yields the `_leftchild` attribute. If the `_leftchild` attribute has its own child candidates, the method will yield those values, and so on. However, if the keys are mutable, such as lists or dictionaries, they cannot be used as keys in a dictionary. This is because the hash value of a mutable object can change if the object is modified, which would cause the dictionary to become corrupted.

If you need to check if a key exists in a dictionary and create a new item if it doesn't, you can use the `setdefault` method. This method returns the value for the given key if the key is already in the dictionary. If the key is not in the dictionary, it adds the key with a default value and returns the default value.
This method is more concise and efficient than creating a new item if the key doesn't exist, and then updating the dictionary.

column1: See the documentation for the built-in .index() method of the list:
Return zero-based index in the list of the first item whose value is equal to x. Raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

The optional arguments start and end are interpreted as in the slice notation and are used to limit the search to a particular subsequence of the list. The returned index is computed relative to the beginning of the full sequence rather than the start argument.

An index call checks every element of the list in order, until it finds a match. If the list is long, and if there is no guarantee that the value will be near the beginning, this can slow down the code.

This problem can only be completely avoided by using a different data structure. However, if the element is known to be within a certain part of the list, the start and end parameters can be used to narrow the search.
The second call is orders of magnitude faster, because it only has to search through 10 elements, rather than all 1 million.
Only the index of the first match is returned

A call to index searches through the list in order until it finds a match, and stops there. If there could be more than one occurrence of the value, and all indices are needed, index cannot solve the problem:
Instead, use a list comprehension or generator expression to do the search, with enumerate to get indices:

column4: # A list comprehension gives a list of indices directly:

# A generator comprehension gives us an iterable object...

column1: It is not possible to sort a dictionary, only to get a representation of a dictionary that is sorted. Dictionaries are inherently orderless, but other types, such as lists and tuples, are not. So you need an ordered data type to represent sorted values, which will be a list—probably a list of tuples.
sorted_x will be a list of tuples sorted by the second element in each tuple. dict(sorted_x) == x.
And for those wishing to sort on keys instead of values:

,To sort a dictionary by value, you can use the built-in function sorted(). However, this function only works on the keys of the dictionary, not the values. If you want to sort the dictionary by value, you can use a list comprehension to create a list of tuples, where each tuple contains the value and its corresponding key. Then, you can use the sorted() function on the list of tuples.
Here is an example of how you can do this:
sorted_by_value = sorted([(v, k) for k, v in d.items()])
This shows that the dictionary has been sorted by value, with all the values that are equal being grouped together.
If you want to sort the dictionary by value in descending order, you can use the reverse=True argument in the sorted() function:
sorted_by_value = sorted([(v, k) for k, v in d.items()], reverse=True)
This shows that the dictionary has been sorted by value in descending order, with all the values that are equal being grouped together.
It's worth noting that the sorted() function returns a list of tuples, not a dictionary. If you want to convert the list of tuples back into a dictionary, you can use the dict() function:
This is the original dictionary, but with the keys and values sorted by value.

column1: It's boilerplate code that protects users from accidentally invoking the script when they didn't intend to. Here are some common problems when the guard is omitted from a script:

• If you import the guardless script in another script (e.g. import my_script_without_a_name_eq_main_guard), then the latter script will trigger the former to run at import time and using the second script's command line arguments. This is almost always a mistake.

• If you have a custom class in the guardless script and save it to a pickle file, then unpickling it in another script will trigger an import of the guardless script, with the same problems outlined in the previous bullet.
To better understand why and how this matters, we need to take a step back to understand how Python initializes scripts and how this interacts with its module import mechanism.
Whenever the Python interpreter reads a source file, it does two things:
• it sets a few special variables like __name__, and then
• it executes all of the code found in the file.
Let's see how this works and how it relates to your question about the __name__ checks we always see in Python scripts.
Let's use a slightly different code sample to explore how imports and scripts work. Suppose the following is in a file called foo.py.
When the Python interpreter reads a source file, it first defines a few special variables. In this case, we care about the __name__ variable.
When Your Module Is the Main Program
If you are running your module (the source file) as the main program, e.g.
the interpreter will assign the hard-coded string "

# of your module when run as the main program.
When Your Module Is Imported By Another

The interpreter will search for your foo.py file (along with searching for a few other variants), and prior to executing that module, it will assign the name "

You might naturally wonder why anybody would want this. Well, sometimes you want to write a .py file that can be both used by other programs and/or modules as a module, and can also be run as the main program itself. Examples:
• Your module is a library, but you want to have a script mode where it runs some unit tests or a demo.

• Your module is only used as a main program, but it has some unit tests, and the testing framework works by importing .py files like your script and running special test functions. You don't want it to try running the script just because it's importing the module.
• Your module is mostly used as a main program, but it also provides a programmer-friendly API for advanced users.
Beyond those examples, it's elegant that running a script in Python is just setting up a few magic variables and importing the script. "

column1:

A list of lists named xss can be flattened using a nested list comprehension:
return [x for xs in xss for x in xs]
As evidence, using the timeit module in the standard library, we see:
<pre class=""lang-bash prettyprint-override""><code>$ python -mtimeit -s'xss=[[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7],[8,9]]*99' '[x for xs in xss for x in xs]'
10000 loops, best of 3: 143 usec per loop
1000 loops, best of 3: 969 usec per loop
$ python -mtimeit -s'xss=[[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7],[8,9]]*99' 'reduce(lambda xs, ys: xs + ys, xss)'
1000 loops, best of 3: 1.1 msec per loop

Explanation: the methods based on + (including the implied use in sum) are, of necessity, O(L**2) when there are L sublists -- as the intermediate result list keeps getting longer, at each step a new intermediate result list object gets allocated, and all the items in the previous intermediate result must be copied over (as well as a few new ones added at the end). So, for simplicity and without actual loss of generality, say you have L sublists of M items each: the first M items are copied back and forth L-1 times, the second M items L-2 times, and so on
column2: total number of copies is M times the sum of x for x from 1 to L excluded, i.e., M * (L**2)/2.

column1: The list comprehension just generates one list, once, and copies each item over (from its original place of residence to the result list) also exactly once.

RAG,How to use a global variable in a function?,"

How do I create or use a global variable inside a function?
How do I use a global variable that was defined in one function inside other functions?

Failing to use the global keyword where appropriate often causes UnboundLocalError. The precise rules for this are explained at UnboundLocalError on local variable when reassigned after first use. Generally, please close other questions as a duplicate of that question when an explanation is sought, and this question when someone simply needs to know the global keyword.

,

You can use a global variable within other functions by declaring it as global within each function that assigns a value to it:
global globvar    # Needed to modify global copy of globvar
print(globvar)     # No need for global declaration to read value of globvar

Since it's unclear whether globvar = 1 is creating a local variable or changing a global variable, Python defaults to creating a local variable, and makes you explicitly choose the other behavior with the global keyword.
See other answers if you want to share a global variable across modules.

,To use a global variable in a function, you can access it from any function. Unlike local variables, which disappear when their function ends, global variables persist from one function call to the next. According to ""Think Python"" (p. 110), it is common to use global variables for flags, which are boolean variables that indicate whether a condition is true. For example, some programs use a flag named `verbose` to control the level of detail in the output.

However, if you try to reassign a global variable, you might be surprised. Python assumes that the variable is local, and under that assumption, you are reading it before writing it. The solution is to declare the global variable before using it:
Similarly, if you want to update a global variable, you need to declare it:
If a global variable refers to a mutable value, you can modify the value without declaring the variable:
However, if you want to reassign the variable, you need to declare it:

In summary, to use a global variable in a function, you need to declare it using the `global` keyword before using or updating it. RAG,"How do I create a directory, and any missing parent directories?","

How do I create a directory at a given path, and also create any missing parent directories along that path? For example, the Bash command mkdir -p /path/to/nested/directory does this.
For older versions of Python, I see two answers with good qualities, each with a small flaw, so I will give my take on it:
As noted in comments and elsewhere, there's a race condition &ndash
column2: if the directory is created between the os.path.exists and the os.makedirs calls, the os.makedirs will fail with an OSError. Unfortunately, blanket-catching OSError and continuing is not foolproof, as it will ignore a failure to create the directory due to other factors, such as insufficient permissions, full disk, etc.

column1: One option would be to trap the OSError and examine the embedded error code (see Is there a cross-platform way of getting information from Python’s OSError):
Alternatively, there could be a second os.path.exists, but suppose another created the directory after the first check, then removed it before the second one &ndash

Depending on the application, the danger of concurrent operations may be more or less than the danger posed by other factors such as file permissions. The developer would have to know more about the particular application being developed and its expected environment before choosing an implementation.
Modern versions of Python improve this code quite a bit, both by exposing FileExistsError (in 3.3+)...
...and by allowing a keyword argument to os.makedirs called exist_ok (in 3.2+).
<pre><code>os.makedirs(""path/to/directory"", exist_ok=True)  # succeeds even if directory exists.

What functionality does the yield keyword in Python provide?

1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "
<pre><code># Here you create the method of the node object that will return the generator
# Here is the code that will be called each time you use the generator object:
# If there is still a child of the node object on its left
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If there is still a child of the node object on its right
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If the function arrives here, the generator will be considered empty
# There are no more than two values: the left and the right children
<pre><code># Create an empty list and a list with the current object reference
# Loop on candidates (they contain only one element at the beginning)
# Get the last candidate and remove it from the list
# Get the distance between obj and the candidate
# If the distance is ok, then you can fill in the result
# Add the children of the candidate to the candidate's list
# so the loop will keep running until it has looked
# at all the children of the children of the children, etc. of the candidate

• The loop iterates on a list, but the list expands while the loop is being iterated. It's a concise way to go through all these nested data even if it's a bit dangerous since you can end up with an infinite loop. In this case, candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) exhausts all the values of the generator, but while keeps creating new generator objects which will produce different values from the previous ones since it's not applied on the same node.
• The extend() method is a list object method that expects an iterable and adds its values to the list.
Usually, we pass a list to it:
But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:
• You don't need to read the values twice.
• You may have a lot of children and you don't want them all stored in memory.

And it works because Python does not care if the argument of a method is a list or not. Python expects iterables so it will work with strings, lists, tuples, and generators! This is called duck typing and is one of the reasons why Python is so cool. But this is another story, for another question...

,To check if a given key already exists in a dictionary, you can use the `in` operator. This method works by searching the keys in the dictionary and returns `True` if the key is found, and `False` otherwise.
Alternatively, you can use the `get` method, which returns the value associated with the key if it exists, and `None` otherwise:

It's worth noting that the `in` operator is more efficient than the `get` method, especially for large dictionaries. This is because the `in` operator uses a hash table to search for the key, while the `get` method has to iterate over the entire dictionary to find the key.
Source: ""Think Python"" by Allen Downey, pages 103-104.

Regarding the requirement that keys must be hashable, the book explains that this is because dictionaries are implemented using a hash table, which maps keys to their corresponding values. The hash function takes a key and returns an integer that represents the location of the key-value pair in the hash table. If the key is mutable, such as a list, modifying the key would change its hash value, which would cause the key-value pair to be stored in a different location in the hash table. This could lead to unexpected behavior or errors.
Source: ""Think Python"" by Allen Downey, pages 108.
Finally, to check if a value exists in a dictionary's values, you can use the `in` operator with the `values()` method:
Source: ""Think Python"" by Allen Downey, page 104.",295,,,,,0.5,0.6483465581420257,5,"['The in operator works on dictionaries , too
column2: it tells you whether something appears as a key in the dictionary ( appearing as a value is not good enough ) .', ""> > > ' one ' in eng2sp True > > > ' uno ' in eng2sp False To see whether something appears as a value in a dictionary , you can use the method values , which returns a collection of values , and then use the in operator : > > > vals = eng2sp.values ( ) > > > ' uno ' in vals"", 'A hash is a function that takes a value ( of any kind ) and returns an integer .', 'If you modify the key and then hash it again , it would go to a different location .', 'I mentioned earlier that a dictionary is implemented using a hashtable and that means that the keys have to be hashable .']","['To check if a given key already exists in a dictionary , you can use the ` in ` operator .', ""Finally , to check if a value exists in a dictionary 's values , you can use the ` in ` operator with the ` values ( ) ` method :   ` ` ` python   d = { ' one ' : 1 , ' two ' : 2 , ' three ' : 3 }   vals = d.values ( )   print('uno ' in vals )"", 'The hash function takes a key and returns an integer that represents the location of the key - value pair in the hash table .', 'If the key is mutable , such as a list , modifying the key would change its hash value , which would cause the key - value pair to be stored in a different location in the hash table .', 'Regarding the requirement that keys must be hashable , the book explains that this is because dictionaries are implemented using a hash table , which maps keys to their corresponding values .']",172,2587,481,5,"['Why must keys be hashable in Python dictionaries?', ""What method is used to check if a value exists in a dictionary's values?"", 'What does the ""in"" operator tell you about the dictionary?', 'What is the relationship between keys and values in a dictionary?', 'How is an item added to a dictionary?']",python,llama3:instruct

column1: See the documentation for the built-in .index() method of the list:
Return zero-based index in the list of the first item whose value is equal to x. Raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

The optional arguments start and end are interpreted as in the slice notation and are used to limit the search to a particular subsequence of the list. The returned index is computed relative to the beginning of the full sequence rather than the start argument.

An index call checks every element of the list in order, until it finds a match. If the list is long, and if there is no guarantee that the value will be near the beginning, this can slow down the code.

This problem can only be completely avoided by using a different data structure. However, if the element is known to be within a certain part of the list, the start and end parameters can be used to narrow the search.
The second call is orders of magnitude faster, because it only has to search through 10 elements, rather than all 1 million.
Only the index of the first match is returned

A call to index searches through the list in order until it finds a match, and stops there. If there could be more than one occurrence of the value, and all indices are needed, index cannot solve the problem:
Instead, use a list comprehension or generator expression to do the search, with enumerate to get indices:

column4: # A list comprehension gives a list of indices directly:
[i for i, e in enumerate([1, 2, 1]) if e == 1]
# A generator comprehension gives us an iterable object...
g = (i for i, e in enumerate([1, 2, 1]) if e == 1)
# which can be used in a `for` loop, or manually iterated with `next`:

column1: The list comprehension and generator expression techniques still work if there is only one match, and are more generalizable.
Raises an exception if there is no match
As noted in the documentation above, using .index will raise an exception if the searched-for value is not in the list:
If this is a concern, either explicitly check first using item in my_list, or handle the exception with try/except as appropriate.

Alternatively, you can use a for loop to traverse the list and find the index of the item:

In this example, `enumerate` is used to iterate over the list and get both the index `i` and the value `cheese`. The `if` statement checks if the current value is equal to the target item `""Edam""`, and if it is, the index is printed.

The time complexity of finding an item in a list using the `index()` method is O(n), where n is the length of the list. This is because the method has to traverse the entire list to find the item. If the list is large, this can be slow.

On the other hand, if the list is sorted, you can use a bisection search (also known as binary search) to find the item in O(log n) time. This is because the bisection search algorithm divides the search space in half with each iteration, reducing the number of comparisons needed to find the item.
Here is an example of a bisection search algorithm:

In this example, the algorithm starts by dividing the search space into two halves and checking if the target item is in the middle of the list. If it is, the algorithm returns the index of the item. If not, the algorithm repeats the process with the appropriate half of the list until the target item is found or it is determined that the item is not in the list.",426,,,,,0.0,0.58044433319775,0,[],[],148,3574,504,5,"['What is unique about the indices in a dictionary compared to a list?', 'What are some ways to remove an element from a list?', 'How do list indices work?', 'How is a for loop used to traverse a list?', 'Approximately how long does it take to find an item in this sequence if it exists?']",python,llama3:instruct

In the given code, the `for` loop iterates over the dictionary `d` and prints each key and its corresponding value.

As for how Python recognizes that it needs to read only the key from the dictionary, it's because the syntax `for key in d:` is specifically designed for dictionaries. This iterator is an internal implementation detail that is not directly accessible to the programmer.

Regarding the `zip()` function, it is used to traverse a list of tuples in the given code snippet. The `zip()` function takes iterables (such as lists or tuples) and aggregates them into tuples. You can read the output from the shell command one line at a time with `readline()` or get the whole thing at once with `read()`.
For example, to launch the `ls` command in Unix, you can use the following code:

column1: It's boilerplate code that protects users from accidentally invoking the script when they didn't intend to. Here are some common problems when the guard is omitted from a script:

• If you import the guardless script in another script (e.g. import my_script_without_a_name_eq_main_guard), then the latter script will trigger the former to run at import time and using the second script's command line arguments. This is almost always a mistake.

• If you have a custom class in the guardless script and save it to a pickle file, then unpickling it in another script will trigger an import of the guardless script, with the same problems outlined in the previous bullet.
To better understand why and how this matters, we need to take a step back to understand how Python initializes scripts and how this interacts with its module import mechanism.

You might naturally wonder why anybody would want this. Well, sometimes you want to write a .py file that can be both used by other programs and/or modules as a module, and can also be run as the main program itself. Examples:
• Your module is a library, but you want to have a script mode where it runs some unit tests or a demo.

• Your module is only used as a main program, but it has some unit tests, and the testing framework works by importing .py files like your script and running special test functions. You don't want it to try running the script just because it's importing the module.
• Your module is mostly used as a main program, but it also provides a programmer-friendly API for advanced users.
Beyond those examples, it's elegant that running a script in Python is just setting up a few magic variables and importing the script. "

,The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is used to ensure that certain code is only executed when the Python script is run directly, rather than being imported as a module. This idiom is commonly used in Python programs that need to perform some action when the script is run, but not when the module is imported.

The `__name__` variable is a built-in variable in Python that is set to `""__main__""` when the script is run directly, and to the name of the module when it is imported. The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement checks whether the script is being run directly, and if so, executes the code inside the `if` statement.

The reason for using this idiom is to avoid executing code that is intended only for the main program, when the module is imported. For example, if you have a script that prints a message when it is run, but you don't want that message to be printed when the module is imported, you would use this idiom to ensure that the message is only printed when the script is run directly.

In the example provided, the code inside the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement prints a message when the script is run. This code would not be executed if the script is imported as a module, because the `__name__` variable would be set to the name of the module, rather than `""__main__""`.
Source: ""Think Python"" by Allen Downey, pages 144, 195.

This code would only print the message when the script is run directly, and not when the module is imported.",266,,,,,0.0,0.6441206526815556,0,[],[],302,2612,359,4,"['What is the value of __name__ when wc is being run as a script?', ""What should you use Python's script mode for?"", 'What does the Python interpreter do when in script mode?', 'Why is it important to ensure that the program Python is trying to run is the one you are editing?']",python,llama3:instruct

Explanation: the methods based on + (including the implied use in sum) are, of necessity, O(L**2) when there are L sublists -- as the intermediate result list keeps getting longer, at each step a new intermediate result list object gets allocated, and all the items in the previous intermediate result must be copied over (as well as a few new ones added at the end). column2: total number of copies is M times the sum of x for x from 1 to L excluded, i.e., M * (L**2)/2.

RAG+CoI,How to use a global variable in a function?,"

How do I create or use a global variable inside a function?
How do I use a global variable that was defined in one function inside other functions?

Failing to use the global keyword where appropriate often causes UnboundLocalError. The precise rules for this are explained at UnboundLocalError on local variable when reassigned after first use. Generally, please close other questions as a duplicate of that question when an explanation is sought, and this question when someone simply needs to know the global keyword.

,

You can use a global variable within other functions by declaring it as global within each function that assigns a value to it:
global globvar    # Needed to modify global copy of globvar
print(globvar)     # No need for global declaration to read value of globvar

Since it's unclear whether globvar = 1 is creating a local variable or changing a global variable, Python defaults to creating a local variable, and makes you explicitly choose the other behavior with the global keyword.
See other answers if you want to share a global variable across modules.
,**How to use a global variable in a function?**

A global variable is a variable that can be accessed from any function. Unlike local variables, which disappear when their function ends, global variables persist from one function call to the next (Think Python, p. 110). Global variables are often used as flags or boolean variables that indicate whether a condition is true (Think Python, p. 110).

To use a global variable inside a function, you can simply access it without declaring it as global. However, if you try to reassign a global variable, you might be surprised. The following example is supposed to keep track of whether the function has been called:
In this case, example2 creates a new local variable named `been_called`, which goes away when the function ends, and has no effect on the global variable.
To reassign a global variable inside a function, you must declare the global variable before you use it:
The `global` statement tells the interpreter that when you say `been_called`, you mean the global variable

RAG+CoI,How do I get a substring of a string in Python?,"

I want to get a new string from the third character to the end of the string, e.g. myString[2:end]. If omitting the second part means 'to the end', and if you omit the first part, does it start from the start?
Python calls this concept ""slicing"" and it works on more than just strings. Take a look here for a comprehensive introduction.

,To get a substring of a string in Python, you can use the slicing operator (`[]`). The syntax is `[start:stop]`, where `start` is the starting index and `stop` is the ending index. If you omit the `start` index, the slice starts at the beginning of the string, and if you omit the `stop` index, the slice goes to the end of the string.
For example, to get a substring from the third character to the end of the string, you can use `myString[2:]`.
According to the textbook, ""If you omit the first index (before the colon), the slice starts at the beginning of the string. If you omit the second index, the slice goes to the end of the string:""
Therefore, `myString[2:]` would get the substring from the third character to the end of the string, which is `'ana'`.

For dictionaries x and y, their shallowly-merged dictionary z takes values from y, replacing those from x.

• In Python 2, (or 3.4 or lower) write a function:
z = x.copy()   # start with keys and values of x

Say you have two dictionaries and you want to merge them into a new dictionary without altering the original dictionaries:
The desired result is to get a new dictionary (z) with the values merged, and the second dictionary's values overwriting those from the first.
A new syntax for this, proposed in PEP 448 and available as of Python 3.5, is
And it is indeed a single expression.
Note that we can merge in with literal notation as well:
It is now showing as implemented in the release schedule for 3.5, PEP 478, and it has now made its way into the What's New in Python 3.5 document.

However, since many organizations are still on Python 2, you may wish to do this in a backward-compatible way. The classically Pythonic way, available in Python 2 and Python 3.0-3.4, is to do this as a two-step process:
z.update(y) # which returns None since it mutates z
In both approaches, y will come second and its values will replace x's values, thus b will point to 3 in our final result.

Don't use what you see in the formerly accepted answer:

In Python 2, you create two lists in memory for each dict, create a third list in memory with length equal to the length of the first two put together, and then discard all three lists to create the dict. In Python 3, this will fail because you're adding two dict_items objects together, not two lists -
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'dict_items' and 'dict_items'
and you would have to explicitly create them as lists, e.g. z = dict(list(x.items()) + list(y.items())). This is a waste of resources and computation power.

Similarly, taking the union of items() in Python 3 (viewitems() in Python 2.7) will also fail when values are unhashable objects (like lists, for example). Even if your values are hashable, since sets are semantically unordered, the behavior is undefined in regards to precedence. So don't do this:
This example demonstrates what happens when values are unhashable:
Here's an example where y should have precedence, but instead the value from x is retained due to the arbitrary order of sets:

This uses the dict constructor and is very fast and memory-efficient (even slightly more so than our two-step process) but unless you know precisely what is happening here (that is, the second dict is being passed as keyword arguments to the dict constructor), it's difficult to read, it's not the intended usage, and so it is not Pythonic.
Here's an example of the usage being remediated in django.
Dictionaries are intended to take hashable keys (e.g. frozensets or tuples), but this method fails in Python 3 when keys are not strings.
TypeError: keyword arguments must be strings
From the mailing list, Guido van Rossum, the creator of the language, wrote:
declaring dict({}, **{1:3}) illegal, since after all it is abuse of

column2: . Personally, I find it more despicable than

column1: It is my understanding (as well as the understanding of the creator of the language) that the intended usage for dict(**y) is for creating dictionaries for readability purposes, e.g.:

Despite what Guido says, dict(x, **y) is in line with the dict specification, which btw. works for both Python 2 and 3. The fact that this only works for string keys is a direct consequence of how keyword parameters work and not a short-coming of dict. Nor is using the ** operator in this place an abuse of the mechanism, in fact, ** was designed precisely to pass dictionaries as keywords.

Again, it doesn't work for 3 when keys are not strings. The implicit calling contract is that namespaces take ordinary dictionaries, while users must only pass keyword arguments that are strings. All other callables enforced it. dict broke this consistency in Python 2:
TypeError: foo() keywords must be strings
This inconsistency was bad given other implementations of Python (PyPy, Jython, IronPython). Thus it was fixed in Python 3, as this usage could be a breaking change.
I submit to you that it is malicious incompetence to intentionally write code that only works in one version of a language or that only works given certain arbitrary constraints.
dict(x.items() + y.items()) is still the most readable solution for Python 2. Readability counts.
My response: merge_two_dicts(x, y) actually seems much clearer to me, if we're actually concerned about readability. And it is not forward compatible, as Python 2 is increasingly deprecated.

{**x, **y} does not seem to handle nested dictionaries. the contents of nested keys are simply overwritten, not merged [...] I ended up being burnt by these answers that do not merge recursively and I was surprised no one mentioned it. In my interpretation of the word "

Assuming two dictionaries of dictionaries, one might recursively merge them in a single function, but you should be careful not to modify the dictionaries from either source, and the surest way to avoid that is to make a copy when assigning values. As keys must be hashable and are usually therefore immutable, it is pointless to copy them:
Coming up with contingencies for other value types is far beyond the scope of this question, so I will point you at my answer to the canonical question on a "
These approaches are less performant, but they will provide correct behavior.

They will be much less performant than copy and update or the new unpacking because they iterate through each key-value pair at a higher level of abstraction, but they do respect the order of precedence (latter dictionaries have precedence)
You can also chain the dictionaries manually inside a dict comprehension:
<pre class=""lang-py prettyprint-override""><code>{k: v for d in dicts for k, v in d.items()} # iteritems in Python 2.7
or in Python 2.6 (and perhaps as early as 2.4 when generator expressions were introduced):
<pre class=""lang-py prettyprint-override""><code>dict((k, v) for d in dicts for k, v in d.items()) # iteritems in Python 2
itertools.chain will chain the iterators over the key-value pairs in the correct order:
z = dict(chain(x.items(), y.items())) # iteritems in Python 2

RAG+CoI,"What does the ""yield"" keyword do in Python?","

What functionality does the yield keyword in Python provide?
For example, I'm trying to understand this code1:
What happens when the method _get_child_candidates is called?
Is a list returned? A single element? Is it called again? When will subsequent calls stop?
1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "
<pre><code># Here you create the method of the node object that will return the generator
# Here is the code that will be called each time you use the generator object:
# If there is still a child of the node object on its left
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If there is still a child of the node object on its right
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If the function arrives here, the generator will be considered empty
# There are no more than two values: the left and the right children
<pre><code># Create an empty list and a list with the current object reference
# Loop on candidates (they contain only one element at the beginning)
# Get the last candidate and remove it from the list
# Get the distance between obj and the candidate
# If the distance is ok, then you can fill in the result
# Add the children of the candidate to the candidate's list
# so the loop will keep running until it has looked
# at all the children of the children of the children, etc. of the candidate

• The loop iterates on a list, but the list expands while the loop is being iterated. It's a concise way to go through all these nested data even if it's a bit dangerous since you can end up with an infinite loop. In this case, candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) exhausts all the values of the generator, but while keeps creating new generator objects which will produce different values from the previous ones since it's not applied on the same node.
• The extend() method is a list object method that expects an iterable and adds its values to the list.
Usually, we pass a list to it:
But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:
• You don't need to read the values twice.
• You may have a lot of children and you don't want them all stored in memory.

And it works because Python does not care if the argument of a method is a list or not. Python expects iterables so it will work with strings, lists, tuples, and generators! This is called duck typing and is one of the reasons why Python is so cool. But this is another story, for another question...

,The `yield` keyword in Python is used to define a function that returns an iterable sequence of values. When called, the function generates and returns a value, which can be used as part of an expression or assigned to a variable. This type of function is called a ""fruitful function"".

In the example provided, the `_get_child_candidates` method uses the `yield` keyword to return a sequence of child nodes that satisfy certain conditions. The function is called repeatedly, and each time it returns the next value in the sequence until there are no more values to return.

When the method is called, it returns a sequence of values, which can be used as part of an expression or assigned to a variable. The caller can use the returned values to process the child nodes, such as extending a list with the node values.

The `yield` keyword is used to define a generator function, which is a special type of function that can be used to generate and return a sequence of values. Generator functions are useful when you need to generate a sequence of values, but you don't need to store the entire sequence in memory.

It's worth noting that the `yield` keyword is different from other keywords in Python, such as `return` or `pass`. The `yield` keyword is used to define a generator function, which is a specific type of function that returns an iterable sequence of values.

,The difference between Python's list methods `append` and `extend` lies in how they modify the list and the type of argument they take.

The `append` method modifies the list by adding a single element to the end of the list. It returns `None`, meaning the original list is modified in place. On the other hand, the `extend` method takes a sequence (such as a list or tuple) as an argument and appends all the elements of that sequence to the end of the original list.
>>> t1.extend(t2)  # appends all elements of t2 to t1
In summary, `append` adds a single element to the end of the list, while `extend` appends all elements of a sequence to the end of the list.

,

some_list[-1] is the shortest and most Pythonic.

In fact, you can do much more with this syntax. The some_list[-n] syntax gets the nth-to-last element. So some_list[-1] gets the last element, some_list[-2] gets the second to last, etc, all the way down to some_list[-len(some_list)], which gives you the first element.
You can also set list elements in this way. For instance:
